children.name: Sitta
children.description: The only birds that routinely walk headfirst down tree trunks, gripping bark with powerful feet and using the bill to lever up bark and extract insects. They wedge nuts in bark crevices to hack them open — hence the name. The Eurasian Nuthatch plasters the entrance of its nest hole with mud to exactly its own body diameter.

children.name: Sitta europaea
children.description: A compact, blue-grey and buff bird that climbs both up and headfirst down tree trunks, wedging seeds in bark to hammer them open. It plasters its nest-hole entrance with mud to the exact width it needs.

children.name: Sitta canadensis
children.description: A small, active nuthatch of North American conifer forests with a distinctive blue-grey back, rusty underparts, and bold black-and-white head pattern. Unlike most nuthatches it is highly nomadic, irrupting southward in large numbers during years when spruce seed crops fail. Smears the entrance of its nest cavity with sticky resin, apparently to deter predators.

children.name: Sitta carolinensis
children.description: The largest North American nuthatch, widespread in broadleaf and mixed forests from Canada to Mexico. Typically seen in pairs year-round as it creeps headfirst down large tree trunks searching for insects in bark furrows. Its nasal 'yank-yank' call is one of the characteristic sounds of eastern North American deciduous forest; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta pygmaea
children.description: One of the smallest nuthatches in the world, found in ponderosa pine forests of western North America from British Columbia to Mexico. Highly social, foraging in noisy, chattering flocks and roosting communally — sometimes over 150 birds sharing a single cavity on cold nights. A specialist of ponderosa pine, dependent on mature forest for nesting cavities. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta pusilla
children.description: A small, brown-capped nuthatch endemic to longleaf and loblolly pine forests of the southeastern United States. One of only a handful of bird species known to use tools — it regularly picks up bark flakes to pry up other bark while foraging. Cooperative breeder, with nest helpers assisting a dominant pair; closely tied to mature pine forest and declining with longleaf pine habitat loss. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta neumayer
children.description: A large, pale grey nuthatch of arid rocky hillsides and gorges from the Balkans and Greece east to Iran and Afghanistan. Unlike forest nuthatches, it rarely climbs trees; instead it forages on rocky cliffs and builds a remarkably complex mud-and-dung nest-flask plastered into rock crevices. Its loud, melodious song echoes through stony Mediterranean scrubland. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta tephronota
children.description: The eastern counterpart of the Western Rock Nuthatch, replacing it across arid rocky terrain from Iran and the Caucasus east through Central Asia to northwestern China. Larger than S. neumayer and with a longer bill; both species build elaborate mud-flask nests on cliff faces. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta krueperi
children.description: Named after German ornithologist Tobias Krüper, who collected extensively in Greece and Turkey. A small, distinctive nuthatch with a chestnut breast patch, found only in mature conifer forests (primarily Brutian and Turkish pine) in southwestern Turkey and the Greek island of Lesbos. IUCN Near Threatened due to logging and degradation of its specialised old pine forest habitat.

children.name: Sitta whiteheadi
children.description: Named after British naturalist John Whitehead, who collected the type specimen on Corsica in 1883. Endemic to the ancient Corsican pine (Laricio pine) forests of the high mountains of Corsica; one of Europe's most restricted-range birds. Its dependence on mature, unlogged Corsican pine renders it Vulnerable, with a total population of around 3,000 pairs. IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Sitta ledanti
children.description: Named after Belgian ornithologist Jean-Paul Ledant, who discovered the species in 1975 — one of the last bird species to be discovered in the western Palearctic. Endemic to the cedar forests of the Taza National Park in northeastern Algeria. Extremely rare (estimated at under 1,000 individuals) and highly vulnerable to logging and fire; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Sitta villosa
children.description: A small nuthatch of north-eastern China, the Korean Peninsula, and adjacent Siberian Russia, inhabiting conifer and mixed forest with a preference for Scots and Korean pine. Sometimes called the Chinese Nuthatch; sedentary throughout its range. Its grey back and pale whitish underparts, with a distinct eyestripe, distinguish it from the Eurasian Nuthatch. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta yunnanensis
children.description: A small, white-faced nuthatch restricted to pine forests in Yunnan province of southwestern China and adjacent Myanmar. Closely resembles the Eurasian Nuthatch but smaller, with a more restricted range confined to elevations between 2,000 and 3,500 m. IUCN Least Concern but poorly studied.

children.name: Sitta przewalskii
children.description: Named after Russian explorer Nikolai Przhevalsky, who led natural history expeditions deep into Central Asia. A small, distinctively marked nuthatch found in Qinling pine forests of Gansu and Shaanxi, China. Once treated as a subspecies of S. villosa; now recognised as a distinct species based on plumage and distributional evidence. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta nagaensis
children.description: A medium-sized nuthatch of oak and conifer forest in the hills of northeastern India (especially Nagaland), Myanmar, and adjacent southwestern China. Distinguished by the chestnut undertail coverts visible when climbing. A highland forest bird found at 1,000–3,000 m. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta castanea
children.description: A widespread nuthatch of tropical and subtropical forests across the Indian subcontinent, from the foothills of the Himalayas south to the Western Ghats. Its rich chestnut underparts and blue-grey back are distinctive; found in broadleaf forest, secondary woodland, and wooded gardens. One of the most familiar nuthatches in Indian lowland bird lists. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta cinnamoventris
children.description: A nuthatch of humid subtropical and montane broadleaf forests from eastern Nepal and Bhutan through Myanmar to Thailand, closely related to the Chestnut-bellied Nuthatch but inhabiting hillier terrain at generally higher elevations. Distinguished by its deeper cinnamon underparts; sometimes treated as a subspecies of S. castanea. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta himalayensis
children.description: A nuthatch of Himalayan oak and rhododendron forests from Pakistan east through India to Yunnan, at elevations from 1,500 to 3,500 m. Named for the white tail spots visible in flight — a useful field feature distinguishing it from other Himalayan nuthatches. Often found in mixed-species feeding flocks. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta leucopsis
children.description: A boldly patterned nuthatch of high-altitude fir and spruce forests in the western Himalayas, from Afghanistan and Pakistan east to western Nepal. Its striking black-and-white face and deep chestnut underparts make it one of the most handsome nuthatches in Asia. Inhabits old-growth conifer forests above 2,000 m. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta victoriae
children.description: A small, poorly known nuthatch endemic to the Chin Hills and adjacent mountains of western Myanmar, at elevations between 2,000 and 3,000 m. Known from very few specimens and rarely observed in the field; restricted to an extremely limited area of montane oak forest. IUCN Vulnerable due to its tiny range and ongoing deforestation.

children.name: Sitta stoliczkana
children.description: Named after Czech-Austrian naturalist Ferdinand Stoliczka, who made extensive natural history collections in Central Asia and northwestern India in the 1860s–70s. A nuthatch of the arid Karakoram and western Himalayan zone in Pakistan and northwestern India, inhabiting montane scrub and sparse conifer forest at high elevations. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta neglecta
children.description: A widespread nuthatch of pine and mixed forests in mainland Southeast Asia, from Myanmar and southwestern China through Thailand and Indochina. Its name ('neglected') reflects that it was long overlooked as a distinct species from S. castanea. Found at lower elevations than most regional congeners; a familiar bird of lowland pine forest. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta formosa
children.description: Arguably the most spectacular nuthatch, with a brilliant pattern of sky-blue and black on the back, contrasting with bright orange underparts and a vivid blue-and-white head. Found in humid broadleaf forests from the eastern Himalayas through northeastern India and Bangladesh to Myanmar and southwestern China, at 1,000–2,800 m. IUCN Least Concern but rare and patchily distributed.

children.name: Sitta magna
children.description: The largest nuthatch in the world, nearly twice the size of the Eurasian Nuthatch. Found in pine forests of southwestern China (Yunnan, Sichuan) and adjacent Myanmar and Thailand, at elevations from 1,500 to 3,000 m. Its massive bill is capable of excavating nest holes in large pine trunks unaided. IUCN Vulnerable due to logging of mature pine forest within its limited range.

children.name: Sitta frontalis
children.description: A strikingly coloured nuthatch with violet-blue upperparts, a distinctive bright red bill, and a velvety black forehead in males. Widespread across tropical and subtropical forests from Sri Lanka and India through mainland Southeast Asia to the Philippines and the Greater Sundas. One of the most adaptable nuthatches, inhabiting diverse forest types from sea level to 2,000 m. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta solangiae
children.description: A distinctive nuthatch with a pale yellow bill found in montane forests of southern China, Vietnam, and Laos. Named in the ornithological tradition; inhabits broadleaf evergreen forest at elevations from 1,000 to 2,500 m. Related to the Velvet-fronted Nuthatch but easily distinguished by its pale bill and different range. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta azurea
children.description: A brilliantly coloured nuthatch of the montane forests of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Java, and Borneo, with vivid azure-blue upperparts, a contrasting black head and wings, and a red bill. Found in mixed-species canopy flocks in subtropical montane forest above 500 m. One of the most visually striking nuthatches in Southeast Asia. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta ramsayi
children.description: A small nuthatch of broadleaf hill forest in Myanmar and northern Thailand, sometimes treated as conspecific with the Burmese Nuthatch (S. neglecta). Inhabits humid hill forest at moderate elevations; distinguished by subtle plumage and size differences from related species. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta oenochlamys
children.description: A medium-sized nuthatch distributed across the Philippine archipelago, including Luzon, Mindanao, and several offshore islands. Its warm rufous-orange underparts distinguish it from the Velvet-fronted Nuthatch, which it replaces in the Philippines. Inhabits primary and secondary montane forest; a frequent member of mixed-species bird flocks. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Sitta sordida
children.description: A small, soberly plumaged nuthatch known from islands of the eastern Indonesian archipelago, including the Sula Islands. Distinguished from congeners by its plain greyish-brown plumage with minimal contrast. One of the least-studied nuthatches; its precise ecology and population status remain poorly known. IUCN Data Deficient.
